subject: Property Law
lesson_title: Easements and Profits a Prendre: Conditions, Acquisition, Prescription, and Extinguishment

questions.question: In Re Ellenborough Park [1956], which court established the four-part test for a valid easement?

options.A: The English Court of Appeal
options.B: The House of Lords
options.C: The Irish Supreme Court
options.D: The Chancery Division

questions.explanation: Re Ellenborough Park [1956] was decided by the English Court of Appeal. Evershed MR delivered the leading judgment setting out the four conditions a right must satisfy to constitute a valid easement.

questions.question: What is the FIRST condition from Re Ellenborough Park for a valid easement?

options.A: The easement must accommodate and serve the dominant tenement
options.B: There must be both a dominant tenement and a servient tenement
options.C: The dominant and servient tenements must be in different ownership
options.D: The right must be capable of forming the subject matter of a grant

questions.explanation: The first condition is that there must be both a dominant tenement (the land that benefits) and a servient tenement (the land that is burdened). An easement cannot exist in the abstract — it must be attached to land.

questions.question: What does the second condition in Re Ellenborough Park — that the easement must 'accommodate and serve' the dominant tenement — require?

options.A: That the easement increases the market value of the dominant tenement
options.B: That the easement is used at least once each year by the dominant owner
options.C: That the easement benefits the land itself, not merely the personal convenience of its current owner
options.D: That the easement relates exclusively to the physical structure of the dominant tenement

questions.explanation: The second condition requires the easement to benefit the land itself, not merely the personal convenience of its owner. A right that benefits a particular person rather than the land fails this condition.

questions.question: Why cannot an owner have a true easement over their own land?

options.A: Because an owner's rights over their own land are governed by adverse possession
options.B: Because an easement must be registered before it can be exercised
options.C: Because self-grants of easements are void under the nemo dat principle
options.D: Because easements require separate ownership of the dominant and servient tenements — a right over your own land is a quasi-easement, not a true easement

questions.explanation: The third condition from Re Ellenborough Park requires the dominant and servient tenements to be owned or occupied by different persons. A person cannot hold an easement over land they own — such a right is only a quasi-easement.

questions.question: In Re Ellenborough Park [1956], what right was held to constitute a valid easement?

options.A: The right of surrounding householders to use a communal garden for recreation and enjoyment
options.B: The right to a general view over the open countryside
options.C: The right to extract turf from a communal green area
options.D: The right of a developer to restrict the use of neighbouring properties

questions.explanation: In Re Ellenborough Park, the Court of Appeal held that the right of householders surrounding a communal garden to use that garden for recreation and enjoyment satisfied all four conditions and constituted a valid easement.

questions.question: Which of the following fails the FOURTH condition from Re Ellenborough Park — that the right must be capable of forming the subject matter of a grant?

options.A: A right of way over a clearly defined footpath
options.B: A right to a general view over an open landscape
options.C: A right to use a communal garden for recreation
options.D: A right to use a defined parking space on the servient land

questions.explanation: A right to a general view over an open landscape fails the fourth condition — it is too vague and uncertain to be capable of forming the subject matter of a grant by deed.

questions.question: In which situation does the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows [1879] operate?

options.A: Where a dominant owner seeks to acquire an easement by prescription over a neighbour's land
options.B: Where a landowner expressly grants a right of way over the whole of their land
options.C: Where a landowner disposes of part of their land and the purchaser claims quasi-easements exercised over the retained land
options.D: Where a landlord's implied easements survive the expiry of a tenant's lease

questions.explanation: Wheeldon v Burrows [1879] applies where a landowner disposes of part of their land. The purchaser acquires by implied grant all quasi-easements that were continuous and apparent and exercised by the vendor over the retained land at the time of the grant.

questions.question: Under the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows, which quasi-easements are impliedly granted to the purchaser of part of a vendor's land?

options.A: All rights exercised over the retained land for at least 12 years before the conveyance
options.B: All rights strictly necessary for physical access to the purchased land
options.C: All rights expressly identified in the conveyance as quasi-easements
options.D: Those that were continuous and apparent and exercised by the vendor at the time of the grant for the reasonable enjoyment of the land conveyed

questions.explanation: Under Wheeldon v Burrows, quasi-easements are impliedly granted if they were: (1) continuous and apparent, (2) used by the vendor at the time of the grant, and (3) reasonably necessary for the enjoyment of the purchased land.

questions.question: What does section 40 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 do in relation to easements?

options.A: It codifies the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows in Irish law, implying quasi-easements into a conveyance of part
options.B: It abolishes the doctrine of lost modern grant for all prescriptive easement claims
options.C: It provides for the discharge and modification of obsolete easements on application to the court
options.D: It establishes a 12-year prescription period for claims under the doctrine of lost modern grant

questions.explanation: Section 40 of the LCLRA 2009 codifies the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows in Irish law, implying into a conveyance of part the quasi-easements exercised by the vendor over the retained land at the time of the grant.

questions.question: What does section 71 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 provide in relation to conveyances of land?

options.A: It establishes a 20-year prescription period for acquiring easements by lost modern grant
options.B: It implies into every conveyance of land all rights and privileges currently enjoyed with the land
options.C: It abolishes the application of the Wheeldon v Burrows rule in Irish law
options.D: It creates a mandatory registration requirement for all prescriptive easements within six months

questions.explanation: Section 71 of the LCLRA 2009 implies into every conveyance of land all rights and privileges currently enjoyed with the land, giving statutory force to existing use rights.

questions.question: When did the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2021 come into force, reforming prescriptive easements?

options.A: 1 January 2021
options.B: 1 June 2021
options.C: November 2021
options.D: 1 December 2021

questions.explanation: The LCLRA 2021 came into force in November 2021. It repealed sections 33 to 39 of the LCLRA 2009 and reinstated the doctrine of lost modern grant as the primary route for acquiring prescriptive easements.

questions.question: Which sections of the LCLRA 2009 were repealed by the LCLRA 2021 in relation to prescription of easements?

options.A: Sections 40 to 50
options.B: Sections 71 to 85
options.C: Sections 20 to 32
options.D: Sections 33 to 39

questions.explanation: The LCLRA 2021 repealed sections 33 to 39 of the LCLRA 2009, which had introduced a new prescription regime. The 2021 Act reinstated lost modern grant with a 20-year period.

questions.question: Following the LCLRA 2021, what is the required period of continuous qualifying use for a prescriptive easement by lost modern grant in Ireland?

options.A: Twenty years
options.B: Twelve years
options.C: Thirty years
options.D: Seven years

questions.explanation: The LCLRA 2021 reinstated lost modern grant as the primary route for prescriptive easement acquisition in Ireland, requiring twenty years of continuous nec vi, nec clam, nec precario use.

questions.question: In the context of prescription, the Latin phrase 'nec vi' means that use must be:

options.A: Without any interruption throughout the prescriptive period
options.B: Without force — not exercised in the face of opposition or protest from the servient owner
options.C: Without any formal legal claim asserted by the claimant
options.D: Without the knowledge of the servient owner at any point

questions.explanation: 'Nec vi' means without force. A prescriptive claim fails if the claimant exercised the right forcibly or in the face of active opposition from the servient owner. Use must be peaceable.

questions.question: In the context of prescription, the Latin phrase 'nec clam' means that use must be:

options.A: Without the consent of the servient owner at any stage
options.B: Without force or opposition from the servient owner
options.C: Without secrecy — open and visible, so that the servient owner knew or ought to have known of the use
options.D: Without any formal written agreement between the parties

questions.explanation: 'Nec clam' means without secrecy. Prescriptive use must be open and visible — exercised in a manner that the servient owner knew or ought reasonably to have known about. Covert use cannot found a prescriptive claim.

questions.question: In the context of prescription, the Latin phrase 'nec precario' means that use must be:

options.A: Without violence or threat of violence at any point
options.B: Without interruption for the full duration of the prescriptive period
options.C: Without any intention to assert a legal claim over the servient land
options.D: Without permission — exercised as of right, not under any licence granted by the servient owner

questions.explanation: 'Nec precario' means without permission. Use that is precario — exercised with the permission of the servient owner — cannot found a prescriptive claim, because permissive use is not use as of right.

questions.question: Why does use with the permission of the servient owner destroy a claim to a prescriptive easement?

options.A: Because permissive use is not use as of right — it is precario, and prescription requires nec precario use throughout
options.B: Because permission converts the use into an express easement, overriding any prescriptive claim
options.C: Because the LCLRA 2009 specifically provides that permissive use cannot found a prescriptive claim
options.D: Because permission interrupts the prescriptive period, requiring the claimant to recommence the 20-year period

questions.explanation: Permissive use is precario — the claimant exercises the right by leave of the servient owner, not as of right. Prescription requires nec precario use. Any permission, even informal or oral, means the nec precario requirement is not satisfied.

questions.question: What did Tehidy Minerals Ltd v Norman [1971] establish regarding abandonment of an easement?

options.A: An easement is automatically abandoned after 20 years of non-use
options.B: Long non-use is evidence of abandonment but not conclusive — the dominant owner must have truly decided not to exercise the right again
options.C: Abandonment requires a formal deed of release executed by the dominant owner
options.D: An easement is abandoned as soon as the dominant owner acquires an alternative means of achieving the same benefit

questions.explanation: Tehidy Minerals Ltd v Norman [1971] established that long non-use of an easement is evidence of abandonment but is not conclusive. For abandonment to be established, the dominant owner must have truly decided, with a clear and definitive intention, not to exercise the right again.

questions.question: Under the principle in Tehidy Minerals v Norman, which of the following would be sufficient to establish abandonment of an easement?

options.A: A period of non-use of the easement for 12 or more years
options.B: A period of non-use of the easement for 20 or more years without explanation
options.C: A substantial period of non-use combined with a clear and definitive intention to relinquish the right permanently
options.D: The dominant owner's sale of the dominant tenement to a purchaser who did not exercise the easement for five years

questions.explanation: Under Tehidy Minerals v Norman, abandonment requires both a substantial period of non-use AND a clear, definitive intention by the dominant owner to relinquish the right permanently. Long non-use alone is not conclusive.

questions.question: How is an easement extinguished by 'unity of seisin'?

options.A: By the dominant owner expressly releasing the easement to the servient owner by deed
options.B: By the servient owner adversely possessing the dominant tenement for 12 years
options.C: By a court order declaring the easement obsolete following a change in circumstances
options.D: Where the same person acquires ownership of both the dominant and servient tenements, the easement is extinguished because a person cannot hold an easement over their own land

questions.explanation: When the dominant and servient tenements come into common ownership and possession of the same person, the easement is extinguished by unity of seisin. A person cannot hold an easement over their own land — the right merges into the ownership.

questions.question: What is required for a valid express release of an easement by the dominant owner?

options.A: A deed executed by the dominant owner
options.B: A written notice served on the servient owner and registered in the Land Registry
options.C: A statutory declaration filed with the Property Registration Authority
options.D: A court order confirming the dominant owner's intention to release the right

questions.explanation: An easement is a legal interest in land and its formal express release requires a deed executed by the dominant owner.

questions.question: What essential feature distinguishes a profit a prendre from an easement?

options.A: A profit a prendre must always be acquired by prescription while an easement may be expressly granted
options.B: A profit a prendre confers a right to take something from the land — such as fish, timber, game, or minerals — whereas an easement confers only a right to use the land
options.C: A profit a prendre requires both a dominant and servient tenement while an easement can exist in gross
options.D: A profit a prendre is a purely personal right that does not pass with the land on a sale

questions.explanation: The key distinction is that a profit a prendre involves the right to enter another person's land and take something from it — turf, fish, game, timber, or minerals. An easement confers only a right to use the servient land.

questions.question: What does it mean for a profit a prendre to exist 'in gross'?

options.A: The profit was acquired through a period of gross (30-year) prescription
options.B: The profit covers a large or substantial area of the servient tenement
options.C: The profit exists independently of any dominant tenement — it is attached to a person rather than to land
options.D: The profit has been registered as a charge against the servient land in the Land Registry

questions.explanation: Unlike an easement, a profit a prendre can exist in gross — meaning it is held independently without reference to any dominant tenement. It is attached to a person and can be transferred without conveying any dominant land.

questions.question: Which of the following rights would be classified as a profit a prendre rather than an easement?

options.A: A right of way across a neighbour's field to reach a public road
options.B: A right to use a shared driveway connecting two properties
options.C: A right to store goods in a neighbour's outbuilding
options.D: A right to catch and take fish from a river flowing through a neighbour's land

questions.explanation: The right to catch and take fish from another's land is a profit a prendre (profit of piscary). It involves taking something (fish) from the servient land. Rights of way and rights to use land are easements, not profits.

questions.question: What is the distinguishing feature of an easement of necessity?

options.A: It arises where, without the easement, the dominant land would be completely landlocked or otherwise entirely inaccessible
options.B: It arises where the easement is commercially necessary for the full economic exploitation of the dominant land
options.C: It arises where the dominant owner has used the right for 20 years in circumstances of practical necessity
options.D: It arises where the express grant of the easement is necessary to complete registered title

questions.explanation: An easement of necessity arises by implied grant where, without the easement, the dominant land would be entirely landlocked or inaccessible. The implied grant gives minimum access necessary to give effect to the conveyance.

questions.question: The second condition from Re Ellenborough Park requires the easement to 'accommodate and serve' the dominant tenement. Which right FAILS this condition?

options.A: A right of way benefiting a farmhouse to reach its surrounding fields
options.B: A right given to a landowner personally to attend the theatre on a neighbouring estate, unconnected with the use of their land
options.C: A right of drainage through pipes running under neighbouring land
options.D: A right to use a shared access road serving a residential development

questions.explanation: A right to attend a neighbouring theatre for personal entertainment fails the accommodation condition — it benefits the individual personally, not the land. The right must benefit the dominant land itself.

questions.question: What does 'continuous and apparent' mean in the context of the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows?

options.A: The right must have been exercised every day without any interruption whatsoever
options.B: The right must have been exercised for at least 12 years before the date of the conveyance
options.C: The right must be discoverable on a careful inspection of the land and must be of a continuing character, as opposed to a one-off or purely sporadic use
options.D: The right must be visible from the public road and known to the general neighbourhood

questions.explanation: 'Continuous and apparent' under Wheeldon v Burrows means the right must be discoverable on a reasonable inspection of the land (apparent) and must be of a continuing rather than episodic character (continuous). A well-worn path would satisfy this; a right exercised on a single occasion would not.

questions.question: A vendor uses a path across his kitchen garden to reach his orchard. He sells the orchard without expressly granting any right of way. Under which principle may the purchaser claim an implied right of way over the kitchen garden?

options.A: The doctrine of lost modern grant under the LCLRA 2021
options.B: Section 71 of the LCLRA 2009, implying all rights currently enjoyed with the land
options.C: The doctrine of non-derogation from grant only
options.D: The rule in Wheeldon v Burrows — the path was continuous, apparent, and reasonably necessary for the enjoyment of the orchard at the time of the grant

questions.explanation: Under Wheeldon v Burrows, when a vendor sells part of their land, quasi-easements that were continuous and apparent and reasonably necessary for the enjoyment of the conveyed land are impliedly granted. The path across the kitchen garden satisfies these conditions.

questions.question: What is the doctrine of 'lost modern grant' as a basis for acquiring a prescriptive easement?

options.A: A legal fiction under which equity presumes that a formal grant of the easement was made in the past, even though no such grant can be found
options.B: A statutory method under which the court declares an easement after 12 years of qualifying use
options.C: A doctrine under the LCLRA 2009 implying easements where a conveyance fails to mention them
options.D: A rule by which a court reinstates a previously extinguished easement where justice requires

questions.explanation: Lost modern grant is a legal fiction. Where the claimant has used a right as of right for a sufficient period, the law presumes a formal grant was made at some point in the past — even though no such grant can actually be found. The LCLRA 2021 reinstated this doctrine with a 20-year qualifying period.

questions.question: What is the effect of section 71 of the LCLRA 2009 on a conveyance of land?

options.A: It requires all easements appurtenant to the conveyed land to be expressly listed in the transfer document
options.B: It implies into every conveyance all rights and privileges currently enjoyed with the land, giving statutory force to existing use rights
options.C: It abolishes all easements that have not been registered at the time of the conveyance
options.D: It restricts prescriptive easement claims to a maximum period of 12 years after any conveyance

questions.explanation: Section 71 of the LCLRA 2009 implies into every conveyance of land all rights and privileges currently enjoyed with the land. This gives statutory force to existing use rights and supplements quasi-easements implied under section 40.

questions.question: A farmer has crossed a neighbour's field for 25 years, but for the first four years he physically broke down a fence the neighbour erected to stop him. Which limb of the 'as of right' requirement has been breached?

options.A: Nec clam — his use was secret during those early years
options.B: Nec precario — his use was with the neighbour's implied permission
options.C: Nec vi — his use was by force in the face of the servient owner's opposition
options.D: None — the farmer's persistence in exercising the right is consistent with use as of right

questions.explanation: Nec vi requires that use be without force. Breaking down a fence erected by the servient owner to stop the use is classic vi — the use was exercised forcibly in the face of active opposition. This defeats the prescriptive claim for those years.

questions.question: How may an easement be expressly created as a legal interest in Irish law?

options.A: By a written agreement signed by both parties, even without a formal deed
options.B: By registration in the Land Registry without any underlying deed
options.C: By a statutory declaration executed by both the dominant and servient owners
options.D: By express grant or express reservation in a deed executed as a formal legal document

questions.explanation: An easement is a legal interest in land and must be created by deed. It can arise by express grant — the grantor conveys the right to another — or by express reservation — the grantor retains the right over land being conveyed away.

questions.question: A claimed right to a 'general view' over a neighbouring landscape fails the fourth condition in Re Ellenborough Park. What is the reason for this failure?

options.A: A general view is too vague and uncertain — not sufficiently defined to be capable of forming the subject matter of a grant by deed
options.B: A view is an incorporeal right and cannot be attached to any land as a servient tenement
options.C: A view fails the accommodation condition because it only serves the personal enjoyment of the occupier
options.D: A view requires a dominant tenement but no dominant tenement can be identified

questions.explanation: A right to a general view fails the fourth condition from Re Ellenborough Park — the requirement that the right be capable of forming the subject matter of a grant. It is too vague, wide, and uncertain to be granted by deed as a defined easement.

questions.question: The right to park a vehicle in a defined space has been recognised as potentially capable of constituting a valid easement. Which condition from Re Ellenborough Park requires the most careful analysis?

options.A: The first condition — whether both a dominant and servient tenement can be identified
options.B: The fourth condition — whether the right is sufficiently defined and not so wide as to oust the servient owner from reasonable use of their land
options.C: The third condition — whether the dominant and servient tenements are in separate ownership
options.D: The second condition — whether parking a car can genuinely accommodate and serve the dominant tenement

questions.explanation: A right to park requires careful analysis of the fourth condition. A right to park in a defined space is more likely to qualify than an undefined general right to park anywhere. The degree of definition and whether the right would oust the servient owner are key considerations.

questions.question: In the context of easements, what is the 'dominant tenement'?

options.A: The land over which the easement is exercised and which carries the burden of the right
options.B: The land retained by a vendor after a sale of part of their estate
options.C: The land that benefits from the easement
options.D: The land whose commercial value was assessed when the easement was originally granted

questions.explanation: The dominant tenement is the land that has the benefit of the easement — the land whose owner is entitled to exercise the right. The servient tenement is the land over which the easement is exercised.

questions.question: In the context of easements, what is the 'servient tenement'?

options.A: The land that benefits from the easement and whose owner may exercise it
options.B: The land belonging to the person who first acquired the easement by prescription
options.C: The land from which profits a prendre are extracted by the dominant owner
options.D: The land that is burdened by the easement — the land over which the right is exercised

questions.explanation: The servient tenement is the land that is burdened by the easement. The easement is exercised over the servient tenement, which must endure the use by the dominant owner.

questions.question: A profit a prendre that is annexed to and passes automatically with a dominant tenement on a sale is called:

options.A: An appurtenant profit
options.B: A profit in gross
options.C: A profit by prescription
options.D: A profit by express reservation

questions.explanation: An appurtenant profit is attached to land and passes automatically when that land is sold. It differs from a profit in gross, which is held by a person independently of any land.

questions.question: The rule in Wheeldon v Burrows concerns which type of easement acquisition?

options.A: Implied reservation — the vendor reserves rights over the land they are selling
options.B: Implied grant — the vendor impliedly grants to the purchaser quasi-easements exercised over the retained land at the time of the sale
options.C: Both implied grant and implied reservation equally
options.D: Express grant — Wheeldon v Burrows only applies where easements are explicitly listed in the conveyance

questions.explanation: Wheeldon v Burrows concerns implied grant to the purchaser. When a vendor sells part of their land, quasi-easements exercised over the retained land are impliedly granted to the purchaser if they satisfy the required conditions. It does not concern reservation of rights by the vendor.

questions.question: Which of the following has been recognised as a valid negative easement — one that prevents the servient owner from doing something?

options.A: A right of way across a defined footpath
options.B: A right to take game and birds from a neighbouring estate
options.C: A right to the support of a building from an adjoining structure
options.D: A right to use a shared communal garden

questions.explanation: The right to support of a building from an adjoining structure is a recognised negative easement — it prevents the servient owner from demolishing or altering their structure in a way that removes the support. Rights of way and garden use rights are positive easements.

questions.question: A claimant uses a pathway across a neighbour's field openly and without force for 23 years. Throughout that time the neighbour has given informal verbal permission each spring. Can the claimant acquire a prescriptive easement?

options.A: Yes — verbal permission is too informal to defeat a prescriptive easement claim
options.B: Yes — the claimant has used the path openly and without force, satisfying nec vi and nec clam
options.C: Yes — the LCLRA 2021 removed the nec precario requirement for lost modern grant claims
options.D: No — use with the servient owner's permission is precario and therefore not as of right; prescription cannot run where use is consistently permissive

questions.explanation: The nec precario requirement means use must be without permission. Where the servient owner has consistently granted permission — even informally and orally — the use is precario, not as of right. Prescription cannot run regardless of how long permissive use continues.

questions.question: Where the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows applies on a sale of part, who obtains the benefit of the implied quasi-easements?

options.A: The purchaser of the part of the land that was conveyed away by the vendor
options.B: The vendor who retains the remainder of the original landholding after the sale
options.C: Both the vendor and the purchaser share the benefit equally
options.D: The mortgagee of the retained land, if any mortgage was created at the time of the sale

questions.explanation: The rule in Wheeldon v Burrows operates in favour of the purchaser — the grantee of the part sold. The purchaser acquires, by implied grant, the quasi-easements exercised by the vendor over the retained land for the benefit of the land conveyed.

questions.question: A servient owner grants an oral licence each year to a neighbour to use a footpath. The neighbour has used the path in reliance on these annual licences for 25 years. Can the neighbour claim a prescriptive easement?

options.A: Yes — after 20 years of continuous use the prescription period is satisfied regardless of licences
options.B: No — the use has always been precario throughout; it was never exercised as of right, so prescription cannot run regardless of its duration
options.C: Yes — oral licences are too informal to constitute permission for the purposes of prescription
options.D: No — the first annual licence restarts the period, but subsequent licences have no effect

questions.explanation: Where the servient owner has consistently granted permission — even oral, informal, annual permission — the use is precario throughout. Prescription requires nec precario use. No prescriptive right can arise no matter how long permissive use continues.

questions.question: Why does unity of seisin extinguish an easement?

options.A: Because the dominant tenement merges physically into the servient tenement under the principle of accretion
options.B: Because the LCLRA 2009 requires automatic deregistration of all easements upon any change of ownership
options.C: Because a person cannot hold an easement over their own land — when the dominant and servient tenements come into common ownership, the right has no legal basis to continue
options.D: Because section 40 of the LCLRA 2009 provides that merger of tenements extinguishes all subsidiary rights automatically

questions.explanation: Unity of seisin extinguishes an easement because a person cannot hold a right over their own land. When the dominant and servient tenements come into common ownership, the easement has no legal basis to exist and is extinguished.

questions.question: Section 40 of the LCLRA 2009 codifies which common law rule in Irish law?

options.A: The rule in Tulk v Moxhay regarding restrictive covenants in equity
options.B: The rule in Tehidy Minerals v Norman regarding abandonment of easements
options.C: Common law prescription requiring use since time immemorial
options.D: The rule in Wheeldon v Burrows, implying quasi-easements into a conveyance of part

questions.explanation: Section 40 of the LCLRA 2009 gives statutory effect to the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows in Irish law, implying into a conveyance of part the quasi-easements that were exercised by the vendor over the retained land at the time of the grant.

questions.question: The LCLRA 2021 repealed sections 33 to 39 of the LCLRA 2009 and reinstated which doctrine of prescriptive easement acquisition?

options.A: Lost modern grant — the legal fiction that a formal grant was made in the past
options.B: Common law prescription — requiring proof of use since time immemorial
options.C: Prescription under the Prescription Act 1832 — the English statutory method
options.D: Prescription by analogy — using evidence of use by a neighbouring dominant tenement

questions.explanation: The LCLRA 2021 repealed the prescription provisions in the LCLRA 2009 and reinstated the doctrine of lost modern grant as the primary method of acquiring prescriptive easements in Ireland, with a 20-year qualifying period of nec vi, nec clam, nec precario use.

questions.question: Under the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows, must the quasi-easement have been exercised by the vendor over the retained land at the time of the disposition?

options.A: No — it is sufficient that the quasi-easement was exercised at any point in the 10 years before the conveyance
options.B: Yes — the quasi-easement must have been used and enjoyed by the vendor at the time of the grant for the benefit of the part being conveyed
options.C: No — it is sufficient that the quasi-easement is reasonably necessary for the purchaser's future intended use of the land
options.D: Yes — but only if the quasi-easement was visible from the public highway at the date of the conveyance

questions.explanation: Under Wheeldon v Burrows, the quasi-easement must have been exercised by the vendor over the retained land at the time of the grant. It must be both continuous and apparent and in use at the date of the conveyance.

questions.question: What does it mean for a prescriptive claim to be exercised 'as of right'?

options.A: That the claimant has obtained a formal legal opinion confirming their entitlement before exercising the right
options.B: That the claimant has registered their prescriptive claim at the Property Registration Authority
options.C: That the use was exercised openly, without force, without secrecy, and without permission — as if the claimant already held the right
options.D: That the claimant asserted the right in writing to the servient owner at the outset of the prescriptive period

questions.explanation: Use 'as of right' means nec vi, nec clam, nec precario — without force, without secrecy, and without permission. The claimant must exercise the right openly and as if they were already entitled to do so.

questions.question: A neighbour crosses your land openly and without force for 22 years. You always considered the crossing to be done with your implied permission but never communicated this. What is the best analysis of the prescriptive claim?

options.A: No prescriptive claim — the neighbour must expressly assert the right before the period runs
options.B: No prescriptive claim — 22 years does not satisfy any recognised prescriptive period
options.C: Valid prescriptive claim — 22 years of open, non-forcible use satisfies the LCLRA 2021 requirements
options.D: This depends on whether the use was truly as of right — an uncommunicated subjective belief in implied permission does not make use precario unless permission was actually operative

questions.explanation: An uncommunicated subjective belief that use is permissive does not make it precario — permission must be operative. If the servient owner never communicated permission, the neighbour may have been using as of right, and 22 years satisfies the 20-year LCLRA 2021 period.

questions.question: Which of the following best states the position regarding acquisition of a right to light as a prescriptive easement in Ireland?

options.A: A right to light can be acquired as a prescriptive easement by 20 years of nec vi, nec clam, nec precario enjoyment under the LCLRA 2021 framework
options.B: A right to light cannot constitute a valid easement because it fails the fourth condition in Re Ellenborough Park
options.C: A right to light can only be acquired by express grant in a deed and never by prescription
options.D: A right to light is classified as a profit a prendre rather than an easement in Irish law

questions.explanation: In Ireland, a right to light is a recognised easement and can be acquired by prescription. Following the LCLRA 2021, the route is lost modern grant — 20 years of nec vi, nec clam, nec precario enjoyment of light across a neighbour's land.

questions.question: Which of the following most accurately states the rule in Tehidy Minerals v Norman [1971] on abandonment of easements?

options.A: An easement is abandoned automatically after 20 years of continuous non-use
options.B: Long non-use is evidence of abandonment but is not conclusive — the dominant owner must have clearly and definitively decided never to exercise the easement again
options.C: Abandonment can be established solely from evidence that the dominant owner sold the tenement to a person unaware of the easement
options.D: An easement is abandoned once the servient owner erects a physical obstruction and the dominant owner takes no legal action within two years

questions.explanation: Tehidy Minerals v Norman [1971] established that long non-use is evidence of abandonment but is not conclusive. To establish abandonment, the court must be satisfied that the dominant owner had a clear and definitive intention to relinquish the right permanently — mere non-use is insufficient alone.
